title: Before the Thunder Counts
synopsis: Hugo loves counting the seconds between lightning and thunder to calculate storm distances, but when a new girl arrives who seems to predict lightning before it even happens, he must solve the mystery of her impossible ability while learning that different minds work in wonderfully different ways.

chapters.title: The Science of Distance
chapters.content:
Lightning flickers across the gray sky, and I start counting. One Mississippi, two Mississippi, three Mississippi, four Mississippi, five Mississippi. Thunder rumbles like a grumpy giant rolling over in bed.

I divide by five in my head. The storm is one mile away, which means I have maybe ten minutes before the real rain starts. Perfect timing for my experiment.

The pebbles under my sneakers are smooth and cold, worn down by a million waves. Some are gray like mouse fur, others speckled like bird eggs. I've arranged them in neat rows of ten because everything makes more sense when it's organized.

Mom thinks I'm weird for coming to the beach during storms, but she doesn't understand. This is when the best science happens. Lightning is electricity jumping from cloud to ground at 224,000 miles per hour, and thunder is the sound that air makes when it gets surprised by all that heat.

I check my stopwatch again. The wind smells like salt and something electric, like when you rub socks on carpet. My hair whips around my face as I scan the horizon for the next flash.

That's when I see her.

illustrations.description: A boy with straight light blonde hair, fair skin, and light blue eyes crouches on a pebble beach, arranging stones in neat rows. He's wearing a small detective-style coat and holding a stopwatch. Storm clouds gather overhead with dramatic shadows falling across the scene. The lighting is film noir style with muted grays and one golden accent of sunlight breaking through the clouds.
illustrations.alt: Hugo organizing pebbles on the beach before a storm

chapters.title: The Girl Who Knows Too Much
chapters.content:
She's sitting on a piece of driftwood about twenty feet away, and here's the weird part: she's wearing sunglasses even though the sky looks like someone spilled ink on gray paper.

I'm about to ignore her and get back to my lightning calculations when she does something impossible. She points at a spot in the sky and says, "There. In three seconds."

I look where she's pointing. Empty gray clouds.

One Mississippi, two Mississippi, three—FLASH!

Lightning splits the sky exactly where she pointed. My mouth falls open like a broken mailbox.

"How did you—" I start to ask, but she's already pointing somewhere else.

"There. Two seconds."

One Mississippi, two—FLASH!

This is scientifically impossible. Lightning doesn't work that way. You can't predict where it's going to strike because electrical charges build up randomly in storm clouds. I read three books about it last month.

"That's impossible," I call out over the wind.

She turns toward me, and even with the sunglasses I can tell she's smiling. "Lots of things seem impossible until you understand them."

I clutch my stopwatch tighter. This girl is ruining everything. I've spent weeks perfecting my storm-distance calculations, and now she shows up acting like she can control lightning with her brain.

"What's your name?" I ask, because solving mysteries starts with gathering data.

"Zara," she says. "And you must be the boy who counts thunder."

illustrations.description: A mysterious girl in sunglasses sits on driftwood pointing at storm clouds, while a boy with straight light blonde hair, fair skin, and light blue eyes stands nearby holding a stopwatch, looking confused and frustrated. The scene has dramatic film noir lighting with heavy shadows and one bright flash of lightning in the distance as an accent color against the muted palette.
illustrations.alt: Zara predicting lightning while Hugo watches in disbelief

chapters.title: Patterns in the Static
chapters.content:
"How do you know about my counting?" I ask, but Zara just shrugs like knowing impossible things is perfectly normal.

The storm is getting closer. I can smell it in the air—that sharp, clean scent that means business. The waves are getting bigger too, crashing against the rocks with more attitude than before.

Zara stands up from her driftwood and walks over to my organized pebbles. She studies them for a moment, then nods like they make perfect sense.

"You see patterns," she says. It's not a question.

"Everything has patterns," I reply, defensive. "Thunder follows lightning. Waves follow tides. Numbers follow rules."

"So does lightning," she says, and that's when my brain does a little flip.

She kneels down next to my pebble arrangements and points to a gap I left in the third row. "You left space for the irregular ones. The pieces that don't quite fit."

I stare at her. Nobody ever notices details like that. Most people just see a weird kid playing with rocks.

"Lightning has patterns too," she continues. "But you have to know how to listen for them."

She takes off her sunglasses, and I see why she was wearing them. Her eyes are different colors—one brown, one green—and they seem to catch light in a way that makes me think of prisms.

"Listen?" I ask.

"To the electricity in the air. It talks before it jumps."

Another flash lights up the sky, and this time I try to listen instead of just count. But all I hear is wind and waves and my own confused breathing.

illustrations.description: A boy with straight light blonde hair, fair skin, and light blue eyes kneels next to neat rows of pebbles while a girl with different colored eyes (one brown, one green) crouches beside him. The scene uses film noir lighting with dramatic shadows cast across the beach. Storm clouds loom overhead with electric tension in the air, rendered in muted grays with flashes of lightning as accent color.
illustrations.alt: Zara and Hugo examining the organized pebbles together

chapters.title: The Sound of Electricity
chapters.content:
"I don't hear anything," I admit, and for some reason that makes Zara smile wider.

"Not with your ears," she says. "With your whole self. Some people can feel earthquakes coming in their bones. Some people can smell rain three days early. I can feel electricity building up."

She sits cross-legged on the pebbles, right in the middle of my organized rows, and normally that would drive me crazy. But somehow it looks right, like she belongs there.

"Try this," she says. "Stop counting with your brain and start feeling with your skin."

This sounds like the kind of hippie nonsense my aunt talks about, but I'm curious enough to try. I close my eyes and let my mind go quiet.

At first, nothing. Just wind and waves and the smell of seaweed.

Then, slowly, I start to notice things. The hair on my arms is standing up slightly. There's a buzzing feeling in the air, like the moment before someone turns on a television. My teeth feel tingly.

"There," Zara whispers. "Do you feel it building?"

I do. It's like the sky is holding its breath.

"Northwest," she says softly. "About forty degrees up. In five seconds."

I keep my eyes closed and point where my skin tells me the electricity is thickest. One Mississippi, two Mississippi, three Mississippi, four Mississippi, five—

FLASH! Thunder booms almost immediately.

I open my eyes and realize I'm pointing exactly where the lightning struck. My arm is still tingling.

"How did I do that?" I whisper.

illustrations.description: A boy with straight light blonde hair, fair skin, and light blue eyes sits with eyes closed, arm pointing toward the sky, while a girl sits nearby among scattered pebbles. The film noir style shows dramatic shadows and a moment of electrical tension before lightning strikes. The scene uses muted grays and blues with one bright flash of lightning as the accent color, creating a mysterious detective atmosphere.
illustrations.alt: Hugo learning to feel electricity in the air

chapters.title: Different Kinds of Smart
chapters.content:
"You've always been able to do it," Zara says, helping me gather my scattered pebbles as the first fat raindrops start to fall. "You just thought you were supposed to use numbers instead of feelings."

I stare at the rocks in my hands. They're still arranged in patterns, but now the patterns feel alive instead of just organized.

"I like numbers," I say, because I don't want her to think I'm going to stop being me.

"Numbers are great," she agrees. "But they're not the only way to understand things. Your brain works differently than other people's brains. That's not weird—that's useful."

The rain is coming down harder now, making tiny drumbeats on the pebbles. We should probably head for shelter, but I don't want this conversation to end.

"How long have you been able to feel lightning?" I ask.

"Always," she says. "My mom says I used to cry before thunderstorms when I was a baby, even when the sky was still blue. She thought I was sick until she figured out the pattern."

"Pattern," I repeat, and something clicks in my brain. "You see patterns too. Just different ones than mine."

Zara grins, and raindrops slide down her face like liquid silver. "Exactly. You organize things with your eyes and your brain. I organize things with my whole nervous system."

I think about this as we pack up my pebbles. All my life, people have told me I'm too focused on details, too obsessed with arranging things, too different. But Zara makes it sound like being different is actually pretty cool.

"Want to come back tomorrow?" she asks. "There's supposed to be another storm."

I nod, because suddenly I have about a million questions about electricity and patterns and different kinds of smart.

illustrations.description: A boy with straight light blonde hair, fair skin, and light blue eyes and a girl collect pebbles together as rain begins to fall on the beach. Both are getting wet but smiling, with dramatic film noir shadows around them. The scene has a detective mystery atmosphere with muted colors and silver raindrops as the accent color against the gray storm.
illustrations.alt: Hugo and Zara gathering pebbles in the rain

chapters.title: The Thunder Between Us
chapters.content:
The next day, I arrive at the beach with my stopwatch and a notebook full of questions. But Zara is already there, sitting in the exact same spot, wearing those sunglasses again.

"I've been thinking," I say, settling down next to her on the pebbles. "About patterns and electricity and different kinds of smart."

"Yeah?" She tilts her head like a curious bird.

"I think we could work together. You feel where the lightning wants to go, and I calculate how far away it is. We'd be like a complete weather prediction team."

Zara takes off her sunglasses and looks at me with those mismatched eyes. "You know what I like about you, Hugo?"

I shake my head.

"You don't try to make me normal. And you don't act like your way is the only right way."

I think about that for a moment. "Normal is boring," I say finally. "Normal people can't predict lightning or calculate storm distances. Normal people probably can't even organize pebbles properly."

This makes her laugh, a sound like wind chimes in a gentle breeze.

The storm clouds are building again, darker and more promising than yesterday. I can feel that electric tingle starting to build in my arms, and I can see Zara getting that focused look that means she's listening to the sky.

"There," she says, pointing. "But this one's different. Bigger."

I close my eyes and feel for it. She's right. The electricity in the air is thicker, more urgent.

"Count with me?" I ask.

"Count with me?" she asks at the same time.

We grin at each other and point at the same patch of sky.

"One Mississippi," we say together, and wait for the thunder to tell us exactly how far we've come.

illustrations.description: A boy with straight light blonde hair, fair skin, and light blue eyes and a girl sit together on a pebble beach, both pointing at the same spot in storm clouds above. They're working as a team, with detective-style film noir lighting creating dramatic shadows around them. The scene has a muted palette with one bright flash of lightning as the accent color, showing their partnership in solving the mystery of the storm.
illustrations.alt: Hugo and Zara working together to predict lightning

lessonPlan.beforeReading: Discuss how people have different strengths and ways of understanding the world
lessonPlan.duringReading: Notice how Hugo and Zara each have unique abilities that complement each other
lessonPlan.afterReading: Talk about times when being different has been helpful or interesting

coverPrompt.description: A moody film noir style illustration showing a boy with straight light blonde hair, fair skin, and light blue eyes standing on a pebble beach. He wears a small detective's trench coat and holds a stopwatch, looking up at storm clouds gathering overhead. Dramatic shadows cast across the beach, with one beam of golden sunlight breaking through as an accent color against the muted grays and blues. Waves crash nearby and lightning flickers in the distance.
coverPrompt.alt: Hugo on a stormy beach holding a stopwatch

questions.question: What does Hugo use to measure how far away lightning is?
questions.explanation: Hugo uses a stopwatch to count the seconds between lightning and thunder, then divides by five to calculate the distance in miles.

questions.question: What makes Zara's eyes special?
questions.explanation: Zara has heterochromia—one brown eye and one green eye—which Hugo notices when she takes off her sunglasses.

questions.question: How does Hugo organize the pebbles on the beach?
questions.explanation: Hugo arranges the pebbles in neat rows of ten because he likes things to be organized and make sense.

questions.question: What can Zara do that seems impossible?
questions.explanation: Zara can feel the electricity building in the air and predict exactly where and when lightning will strike.

questions.question: How does Hugo learn to feel electricity in the air?
choices: By closing his eyes and using his skin
questions.explanation: Zara teaches Hugo to stop counting with his brain and start feeling with his whole body, including his skin.

questions.question: What does Hugo realize about being different?
questions.explanation: Hugo learns that having a brain that works differently isn't weird—it's useful and gives him special abilities.

questions.question: Why do Hugo and Zara work well together?
questions.explanation: Hugo's ability to calculate and Zara's ability to predict lightning complement each other, making them a great team.
